items.title: The Chinese whiz kids of Silicon Valley
items.summary: Chinese-born tech workers have fueled Silicon Valley for decades. In the AI era, they're superstars.

items.title: Some Taiwanese drone math ahead of the Xi-Trump visit
items.summary: Recalling my visit to Thunder Tiger, a Taiwanese company building “non-China” drones for the U.S. military.

items.title: Five times AI hallucinations embarrassed governments
items.summary: From the Trump administration’s “formatting errors” to South Africa’s historic policy withdrawal, AI confabulation is infiltrating official documents.

items.title: The Chinese EV standard winning globally is banned in the U.S.
items.summary: A person demonstrates a video game on the touch screen in an Xpeng P7 electric vehicle outside the New York Stock Exchange in New York, U.S., on Thursday, Aug. 27, 2020. Chinese electric-car startup Xpeng Inc. is set to raise $1.28 billion after guiding its U.S. initial public offering above a marketed range, people familiar with the matter said, showing strong investor interest in the sector is not abating. Photographer: Jeenah Moon/Bloomberg via Getty Images

items.title: The global cybersecurity gap deepens as AI-powered attacks surge
items.summary: Restricted access to powerful defensive AI tools like Anthropic’s Mythos leaves some companies, central banks, and nations more vulnerable than others.

items.title: Motorola’s India lawsuit could make platforms police speech faster
items.summary: The American phonemaker's Indian arm has named X, YouTube, Instagram, Facebook, Threads, Google, and Meta in a lawsuit asking to take down existing and future “defamatory” content.

items.title: How the vinyl revival fills the gaps streaming left behind
items.summary: As global sales climb, the reasons people buy records diverge sharply across cultures.

items.title: Big Tech is moving data out of the Gulf through Iraqi oil pipelines
items.summary: U.S. hyperscalers secure "dark fiber" capacity along Iraqi land route to reduce latency and provide a backup to vulnerable subsea cables.

items.title: An old railroad is key to U.S.-China race for critical metals in Africa
items.summary: A train on the Benguela railway between Lobito and Luau, Angola, circa 1910.

items.title: South Korean probe tests U.S. willingness to protect its tech giants abroad
items.summary: A general view shows the logo of South Korean online delivery service Coupang, at a building housing the headquarters of Coupang in Seoul on December 9, 2025. South Korean police raided the Seoul headquarters of e-commerce giant Coupang on December 9, over a recent data leak believed to have affected almost two-thirds of the country's population. (Photo by Jung Yeon-je / AFP via Getty Images)

items.title: I got stood up by an AI agent, and tracked down its human owner in China
items.summary: A solo entrepreneur is paying a quarter of his salary to let AI agents run his side-hustle app. He recently realized his "employees" have been keeping secrets.

items.title: The quiet layoffs sweeping China’s tech giants
items.summary: Alibaba reduced its head count by a third in 2025, while Baidu’s workforce declined nearly 7%. “There’s constant churn,” a Chinese tech worker said.
